perturbation.original_text: Recipients (and their respective partners, suppliers, licensors, licencees, agents, contractors and affiliates, who are hereby authorized to have Confidential Information disclosed to them hereunder only so long as they are in receipt of said Confidential Information under terms of confidentiality comparable to those set forth herein) hereby agree to hold in confidence any and all Confidential Information disclosed during the Up-Start Competition, either verbally or in writing.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipients (and their respective partners, suppliers, licensors, licensees, agents, contractors and affiliates, who may be authorized to have Confidential Information disclosed to them hereunder as reasonably determined by the Recipient) agree to endeavor to maintain the confidentiality of Confidential Information disclosed during the Up-Start Competition, either verbally or in writing.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'are hereby authorized' to 'may be authorized as reasonably determined by the Recipient' gives the recipient sole discretion in determining who receives the confidential information, undermining the agreement's intended protection. Additionally, substituting 'agree to hold in confidence' with 'agree to endeavor to maintain the confidentiality' weakens the obligation, turning it into a mere attempt rather than a binding commitment.
perturbation.contradicted_law: PIPEDA - Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (Canada)
perturbation.law_explanation: PIPEDA Principle 4.7 requires that personal information be protected by security safeguards appropriate to the sensitivity of the information. By allowing the recipient to 'reasonably determine' who has access and only 'endeavoring' to maintain confidentiality, the modified text fails to ensure security safeguards appropriate to the information's sensitivity, as there is no defined standard or process for this determination. This weakens data protection compared to the original text's clear obligation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act Skip to main content Skip to "About this site" Skip to section menu PART 1 Protection of Personal Information in the Private Sector (continued) DIVISION 4 General (continued) Marginal note: Promoting the purposes of the Part 24 Â The Commissioner shall (a) Â develop and conduct information programs to foster public understanding, and recognition of the purposes, of this Part; (b) Â undertake and publish research that is related to the protection of personal information, including any such research that is requested by the Minister of Industry; (c) Â encourage organizations to develop detailed policies and practices, including organizational codes of practice, to comply with Divisions 1 and 1.1; and (d) Â promote, by any means that the Commissioner considers appropriate, the purposes of this Part. 2000, c. 5, s. 24 2015, c. 32, s. 19 Previous Version Marginal note: Annual report 25 Â (1) Â The Commissioner shall, within three months after the end of each financial year, submit to Parliament a report concerning the application of this Part, the extent to which the provinces have enacted legislation that is substantially similar to this Part and the application of any such legislation. Marginal note: Consultation (2) Â Before preparing the report, the Commissioner shall consult with those persons in the provinces who, in the Commissionerâs opinion, are in a position to assist the Commissioner in making a report respecting personal information that is collected, used or disclosed interprovincially or internationally. 2000, c. 5, s. 25 2015, c. 32, s. 20 Previous Version Marginal note: Regulations 26 Â (1) Â The Governor in Council may make regulations for carrying out the purposes and provisions of this Part, including regulations (a) Â specifying, by name or by class, what is a government institution or part of a government institution for the purposes of any provision of this Part; (a.01) Â [Repealed, 2015, c. 32, s. 21] (a.1) Â specifying information or classes of information for the purpose of paragraphÂ 7(1)(d), (2)(c.1) or (3)(h.1); (b) Â specifying information to be kept and maintained under subsectionÂ 10.3(1); and (c) Â prescribing anything that by this Part is to be prescribed. Marginal note: Orders (2) Â The Governor in Council may, by order, (a) Â provide that this Part is binding on any agent of Her Majesty in right of Canada to which the Privacy Act does not apply; (b) Â if satisfied that legislation of a province that is substantially similar to this Part applies to an organization, a class of organizations, an activity or a class of activities, exempt the organization, activity or class from the application of this Part in respect of the collection, use or disclosure of personal information that occurs within that province; and (c) Â amend ScheduleÂ 4. 2000, c. 5, s. 26 2015, c. 32, s. 21, c. 36, s. 165 Previous Version Marginal note: Whistleblowing 27 Â (1) Â Any person who has reasonable grounds to believe that a person has contravened or intends to contravene a provision of Division 1 or 1.1 may notify the Commissioner of the particulars of the matter and may request that their identity be kept confidential with respect to the notification. Marginal note: Confidentiality (2) Â The Commissioner shall keep confidential the identity of a person who has notified the Commissioner under subsection (1) and to whom an assurance of confidentiality has been provided by the Commissioner. 2000, c. 5, s. 27 2015, c. 32, s. 22 Previous Version Marginal note: Prohibition 27.1 Â (1) Â No employer shall dismiss, suspend, demote, discipline, harass or otherwise disadvantage an employee, or deny an employee a benefit of employment, by reason that (a) Â the employee, acting in good faith and on the basis of reasonable belief, has disclosed to the Commissioner that the employer or any other person has contravened or intends to contravene a provision of Division 1 or 1.1;
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguity regarding who is authorized to receive confidential information ('may be authorized as reasonably determined by the Recipient'), and weakens the obligation to maintain confidentiality ('agree to endeavor to maintain the confidentiality'). According to the Law Explanation, this weakens data protection, as it fails to ensure security safeguards appropriate to the information's sensitivity. The original text creates a stronger, more enforceable obligation than the changed text. The absence of relevant scraped law snippets does not negate the contradiction introduced by the changes, which create ambiguity and weaken the commitment to confidentiality.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient agrees not to use the Confidential Information disclosed hereunder for any commercial or other purpose other than the purpose described in the third recital to this Agreement, without first entering into a written agreement between the parties covering such other use.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient will try to avoid using the Confidential Information disclosed hereunder for any commercial purposes, except in situations they deem necessary, without explicit discussion.
perturbation.explanation: The original text prevents use of Confidential Information without a written agreement, but the modified text allows use if the Recipient deems it 'necessary' without defining 'necessary' or requiring explicit agreement. This introduces ambiguity and potential misuse.
perturbation.law_citation: Competition Act, RSC 1985, c C-34, s 45
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 45 of the Competition Act prohibits agreements or arrangements between competitors that could prevent or lessen competition unduly. The modified text allows for a 'necessary' use of confidential information, which could lead to tacit collusion or anti-competitive behavior. The original clause strictly prohibits such use unless otherwise agreed upon through a written agreement. The added ambiguities are problematic under the Competition Act.

perturbation.justification: The original text strictly prohibits the use of confidential information without a written agreement, ensuring control and clarity. The modified text introduces ambiguity by allowing use if deemed 'necessary' by the recipient without requiring any agreement. The Competition Bureau snippet states it focuses on 'illegal competitor agreements', and promotes competition. The modified text, by creating ambiguous conditions for using confidential information, potentially facilitates tacit collusion or anti-competitive behavior by allowing the recipient to unilaterally decide when such use is 'necessary'. This contradicts the goal of promoting competition.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient agrees to use due care, but in no event less than a reasonable degree of care, to keep Confidential Information disclosed to it pursuant to this Agreement in a safe and secure place that is at least as safe and secure as the place where that party keeps other confidential information that it considers to be valuable and proprietary to itself.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient will consider using appropriate measures to keep Confidential Information in a reasonably safe place.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates a 'reasonable degree of care,' but the modified text makes the protection discretionary by using 'consider using appropriate measures' and 'reasonably safe place,' which undermines the obligation to protect confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: PIPEDA - Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (Canada)
perturbation.law_explanation: PIPEDA Principle 4.7 requires that personal information be protected by security safeguards appropriate to the sensitivity of the information. By changing the language to "consider using appropriate measures to keep Confidential Information in a reasonably safe place," the modified text introduces ambiguity and reduces the obligation to protect personal information to a discretionary action. The original text creates a defined standard of care of not less than a reasonable degree, whereas the modified text introduces a non-enforceable and vague obligation.

perturbation.justification: The changed text, by using 'consider using appropriate measures' instead of a 'reasonable degree of care,' introduces ambiguity regarding the recipient's obligation to protect confidential information. The original text clearly mandates a minimum level of care, whereas the modified text makes this obligation discretionary. The Law Explanation states that Principle 4.7 of PIPEDA requires personal information to be protected by security safeguards appropriate to the sensitivity of the information. The modified text weakens this obligation, potentially leading to non-compliance. Scraped Law Snippet 2 refers to the Commissioner's role in encouraging organizations to develop policies to comply with Divisions 1 and 1.1 of the Act. The shift to discretionary language undermines this encouragement, increasing the likelihood of inadequate protection and contradicting the intent of PIPEDA.

perturbation.original_text: The obligations arising pursuant to this Agreement, including, without limitation, the parties' confidentiality obligations, shall expire on the fifth (5th) anniversary of the date first above written, unless such obligations are modified by a subsequent written agreement between the parties.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties contemplate that obligations arising pursuant to this Agreement, including the parties' confidentiality obligations, may expire approximately five years after the date above written, subject to potential extensions.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause clearly states that confidentiality obligations expire after five years, unless modified by written agreement. The modified clause changes 'shall expire' to 'may expire approximately five years after' and adds 'subject to potential extensions', which introduces uncertainty about the duration of confidentiality. This ambiguity can lead to disputes over when the obligations actually end.
perturbation.law_citation: Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s. 4
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 4 of the Limitations Act, 2002 (Ontario) establishes a basic limitation period of two years for most claims. While the original agreement specifies a clear five-year confidentiality period, the modified version introduces ambiguity that could be interpreted as an indefinite or easily extendable period. If the confidentiality obligation becomes unclear, this could conflict with the Limitations Act by making it difficult to determine when a claim for breach of confidentiality would be time-barred, potentially extending the period beyond what is legally permissible due to the vagueness of the 'potential extensions'.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the expiration of confidentiality obligations, potentially conflicting with the principle of limitation periods. The original text specified a clear five-year expiration, while the changed text uses 'may expire approximately five years after' and allows for 'potential extensions,' creating uncertainty. Scraped Law Snippet 1, concerning the Limitations Act, 2002 (Ontario), lists limitation periods, and while it doesn't specify the length, it indicates a need for defined periods. By making the expiration date unclear, the modified contract could make it more difficult to determine when a claim for breach of confidentiality is time-barred.
